caveats: a: BLS occupational data cover NAICS 541800 rather than only 541810.
a: Tool use does not imply full task automation.
a: Exposure varies materially by client category, channel mix, data access, and seniority.
rho: Survey populations include brands and publishers as well as agencies.
rho: Stated plans may not be realized.
rho: Integration can mean assistance rather than end-to-end automation.
e: No representative firm-level dataset directly measures the share meeting every control, transferability, recurrence, concentration, and normalized-EBITDA screen.
e: The frozen firm count is itself bridged from revenue size classes using a 15.65% margin.
s5: Gallup covers all U.S. employer businesses, not agencies, and plans are not completed sales.
s5: M&A reports cover a broader marketing-services universe and include deals outside the target EBITDA band.
q: Evidence is not representative of private lower-middle-market contract terms.
q: Capture depends on renewal timing, channel mix, differentiation, measurable outcomes, and whether the agency sells deliverables, capacity, or performance.
d5: BLS covers broader NAICS 541800 and a ten-year horizon.
d5: IAB spending is nominal buyer media spend rather than agency net revenue.
d5: WPP is a large global company rather than a U.S. lower-middle-market sample.
o: Buyer surveys skew toward larger advertisers and rapidly changing digital-video workflows.
o: Smaller clients may lack internal talent, while large clients may have stronger in-housing economics.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Outsourced service for business, nonprofit, and public-sector advertisers, monetized through recurring retainers, project or scope fees, media commissions, and performance incentives; the qualifying lens requires recurring or repeat revenue and normalizes pass-through billings.
lens.deviation_from_default: None; the default U.S. lower-middle-market recurring outsourced-service lens is coherent for full-service NAICS 541810 agencies.
lens.excluded_activities: Shell, captive, and non-control situations; pure media representatives, graphic-design studios, direct-mail operators, marketing consultancies, and ad-tech hosting businesses outside NAICS 541810; project-only, founder-dependent, highly client-concentrated, or nonassignable-contract firms without transferable recurring economics; and media or production pass-through amounts when assessing normalized agency economics.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market full-service advertising agencies with $1-10 million of normalized EBITDA and recurring or repeat outsourced client work across strategy, creative, account management, content production, and media planning or buying.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: occupation mix for NAICS 541800 and documented AI use cases across the advertising campaign lifecycle. Transformation: mapped research, analytics, planning, content generation, design iteration, reporting, optimization, and routine administration to exposed hours; applied lower exposure to relationship management, negotiation, strategic judgment, and accountable approval; then bounded for the narrower full-service-agency population. Error direction: the broader industry includes public relations and media-representation roles that may differ in exposure, so bias can run either way; observed AI use cases may overstate autonomous substitution and therefore tend to bias the estimate upward.
caveats: BLS occupational data cover NAICS 541800 rather than only 541810.
Tool use does not imply full task automation.
Exposure varies materially by client category, channel mix, data access, and seniority.
a.change_evidence: IAB reports that AI can already build media plans, create audience segments, select partners, forecast performance, and support attribution; video advertisers also use generative AI for rapid, lower-cost creative variation. BLS occupation shares show that the broader advertising and public-relations services workforce is concentrated in arts/design/media, sales, management, business analysis, and office support, enabling an hours-based mapping rather than treating every job as equally exposed.
a.rationale: A large share of agency hours sits in research, reporting, media planning, creative production, copy and design iteration, campaign versioning, optimization, and administration. Those activities are increasingly machine-assistable, while brand strategy, senior creative judgment, client stewardship, negotiation, and final approval remain more human-intensive.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS real-output projection for NAICS 541800, IAB nominal advertising-spend outlook, and recent large-agency organic revenue performance. Transformation: converted the BLS 2.7% real annual output projection into an approximate five-year multiplier, then discounted the center for the narrower agency population's in-housing and automation risk and used spend growth and recent revenue contraction to set asymmetric bounds. Error direction: the broader category and aggregate output projection may overstate demand retained by independent agencies; using real rather than nominal output limits upward inflation bias.
caveats: BLS covers broader NAICS 541800 and a ten-year horizon.
IAB spending is nominal buyer media spend rather than agency net revenue.
WPP is a large global company rather than a U.S. lower-middle-market sample.
d5.change_evidence: BLS projects real output for NAICS 541800 to grow 2.7% annually from 2024 to 2034, implying roughly 1.14 over five years, while employment grows only 0.2% annually. IAB expects continued ad-spend growth, but WPP reported recent organic revenue declines and client assignment losses, directly supporting downside dispersion.
d5.rationale: Underlying advertising demand and real industry output can grow over five years, but agency service demand will lag headline media-spend growth where platforms automate buying, brands internalize production, or clients reduce intermediaries. The base therefore sits below the broader industry's mechanical real-output projection.

caveats: No representative firm-level dataset directly measures the share meeting every control, transferability, recurrence, concentration, and normalized-EBITDA screen.
The frozen firm count is itself bridged from revenue size classes using a 15.65% margin.
e.change_evidence: The official industry definition confirms an integrated outsourced scope, while large-agency disclosures document recurring retainers as well as fee, commission, and incentive arrangements.
e.rationale: NAICS 541810 is intrinsically an outsourced-service category, and full-service agencies commonly combine repeat account management, planning, creative, production, and media work. Eligibility is reduced for project-only work, severe customer concentration, founder dependence, nonassignable relationships, and economics dominated by pass-through billings.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $33,005.0M / sectoral output (T30) $68,557.8M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $303,923 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541810); class <5: 9,620 firms, avg revenue/firm $521,704 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $81,647 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,690 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,849,936 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $289,515 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,101 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,849,742 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $602,485 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 977 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,049,911 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,885,811 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 198 firms, avg revenue/firm $48,263,556 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,553,246 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 96 firms, avg revenue/firm $267,062,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $41,795,242 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,175

o.bridge: Source population/quantity: advertiser self-service and in-housing plans, demand for human partner support, and brand preferences for internal, external, or hybrid agentic deployment. Transformation: translated the balance of in-housing signals and continued partner demand into the share of AI-enabled work expected to remain with an external full-service agency rather than move directly to clients or platforms. Error direction: large-advertiser survey samples likely overstate in-housing relative to lower-middle-market clients, biasing orchestration retention downward; agency self-reporting can bias retained relevance upward.
caveats: Buyer surveys skew toward larger advertisers and rapidly changing digital-video workflows.
Smaller clients may lack internal talent, while large clients may have stronger in-housing economics.
o.change_evidence: Half of surveyed agencies feared brands would use AI to reduce agency reliance, and 74% of video buyers had built or planned internal teams for self-service connected-TV activation. Countervailing evidence is that more than 80% still wanted human assistance, and 2026 survey data show brands favoring hybrid or external-partner agentic deployment alongside internal builds.
o.rationale: AI enables brands to internalize more creative variation, planning, activation, and analytics, but full-service agencies retain an orchestration role across brand strategy, cross-channel execution, specialized talent, governance, partner coordination, and accountable client service. Hybrid delivery is therefore more likely than complete bypass for many target firms.

caveats: Evidence is not representative of private lower-middle-market contract terms.
Capture depends on renewal timing, channel mix, differentiation, measurable outcomes, and whether the agency sells deliverables, capacity, or performance.
q.change_evidence: Agency disclosures show retainers, commissions, fees, and performance incentives, but also state that labor costs are the primary input for many fee arrangements. Industry surveys show both growing AI capability and client interest in reducing agency reliance.
q.rationale: Agencies can retain part of AI-created value through broader scope, faster iteration, improved performance, and fixed or value-linked fees. Retainers and incentive structures support capture, but labor-based pricing, procurement, competitive rebids, in-housing, and transparent tool costs pass a substantial share of efficiency gains to clients.

rho.bridge: Source population/quantity: current and planned AI integration reported by U.S. advertising buyers, agencies, publishers, and executives, plus agentic deployment preferences. Transformation: converted current full-lifecycle adoption, near-term plans, use-case breadth, and governance barriers into the share of technically exposed agency hours expected to be practically automated or materially augmented within five years. Error direction: stated adoption plans and vendor-era enthusiasm tend to bias realization upward; rapid improvement in tools and competitive pressure could make the estimate conservative.
caveats: Survey populations include brands and publishers as well as agencies.
Stated plans may not be realized.
Integration can mean assistance rather than end-to-end automation.
rho.change_evidence: Only 30% of surveyed organizations had fully integrated AI across the campaign lifecycle in 2025, but about half of those not fully integrated expected to do so by 2026. Generative-video adoption is already broad, while data-quality and governance gaps and frequent AI incidents provide direct evidence of adoption friction.
rho.rationale: Five years is sufficient for agencies to diffuse proven AI tools through planning, targeting, content production, reporting, and workflow orchestration, but realized adoption will remain below technical exposure because client approvals, fragmented data, rights, governance, brand safety, and integration constrain deployment.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners and observed marketing-services M&A activity. Transformation: used the 22% all-industry transfer intention as an anchor, adjusted for active sector buyer demand and agency owner demographics, and widened bounds for completion rates, internal transfers, minority deals, and target-quality screens. Error direction: intentions and broad deal counts tend to overstate completed qualifying control sales; an aging owner base and strong strategic demand could make the low case conservative.
caveats: Gallup covers all U.S. employer businesses, not agencies, and plans are not completed sales.
M&A reports cover a broader marketing-services universe and include deals outside the target EBITDA band.
s5.change_evidence: Gallup finds that 22% of employer-business owners plan to sell or transfer within five years and 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older. Capstone reports rising marketing-services deal activity through early 2026, with strategic buyers representing most transactions and buyer preference for recurring, low-concentration, AI-capable businesses.
s5.rationale: Owner aging and stated five-year transfer plans create a meaningful seller pool, and current marketing-services transaction activity confirms buyer depth. Realized control sales remain below stated intentions because some owners delay, transfer internally, sell minorities, or fail quality and valuation screens.

principal_driver: The principal driver is the agency's ability to convert automation of routine delivery into more client scope and throughput while preserving its role as the trusted, accountable orchestrator.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is limited evidence that independent lower-middle-market agencies can retain AI-driven savings when clients can demand lower fees, self-serve through platforms, or bring production and activation in-house.

caveats: The a mapping is directionally supported but not reproducible as an hours calculation: BLS covers the broader NAICS 541800 population, O*NET covers one occupation, and the packet does not provide task weights. PROXY is nevertheless the honest state, and the range already spans an H low case of 5.08, so this does not establish a base-tier or action change.

Most AI adoption and orchestration evidence comes from broad advertising-industry or digital-video buyer samples rather than U.S. lower-middle-market full-service agencies and their clients. This population gap is disclosed and appropriately treated as PROXY evidence.

S7 directly supports agentic deployment choices and advertising-spend growth but only indirectly informs q; no cited source measures the share of AI-created value retained by private agencies. q is honestly labeled ESTIMATE, and the MODEL_CONDITIONED action already reflects that weakness.

The s5 base of 0.25 sits above Gallup's all-industry five-year employer-owner transfer intention of 0.22 even though the rationale says realized qualifying control sales fall below intentions. Sector deal activity is buyer-demand evidence rather than an agency-specific completion rate. This inconsistency is not material under the required test because the registered low s5 of 0.16 still leaves F at 7.67 and does not change the HIGHEST_PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The d5 bridge discounts the BLS demand anchor partly for in-housing and platform automation, while o separately discounts work that moves to clients or platforms. That creates some conceptual overlap within D. Removing the overlap would tend to raise d5 and D from the registered base rather than lower the tier, and would not change the action.

The strict eligibility estimate e and value-capture estimate q lack representative lower-middle-market firm or contract data. Their estimate labels, ranges, narrative weakness, and MODEL_CONDITIONED action make the uncertainty visible rather than presenting it as observed evidence.

sources_audited.note: The 2022 NAICS Manual defines 541810 as full-service advertising agencies covering advice, creative, account management, content production, media planning, and buying, and confirms the stated adjacent-industry exclusions. It supports the lens and scope used in the affected primitives, not their numerical values by itself.

The exact BLS May 2023 NAICS 541800 page was retrievable through its indexed official result despite a direct page-render cache error. It reports industry occupational employment shares, including substantial arts/design/media and sales employment, and clearly identifies the broader 541800 population.

O*NET lists market-research tasks including data collection and analysis, reporting, forecasting, effectiveness measurement, and advertising-needs identification, supporting the task mapping used for a.

IAB reports a survey of more than 500 buy- and sell-side experts, 30% full campaign-lifecycle AI integration, half of non-integrators expecting integration by 2026, the cited planning and measurement use cases, and agency concern about client in-housing.

The IAB article reports a 125-executive U.S. survey, broad current GenAI use, 70% experiencing at least one AI incident, and substantial governance and accountability gaps, supporting adoption friction in rho.

IAB reports 86% of buyers using or planning GenAI video creative, rapid and lower-cost variation, 74% building or planning in-house self-serve CTV teams, and more than 80% wanting human sell-side assistance. The last statistic concerns sell-side platform partners rather than advertising agencies, a population limitation disclosed in the record.

The 2026 IAB Outlook supports the stated U.S. ad-spend outlook, agentic use cases, and hybrid or external deployment preferences among 205 buy-side decision-makers. It is only indirect evidence for the numerical value-retention estimate q and does not measure private-agency capture.
BLS Table 2.11 reports NAICS 541800 employment growth of 0.2% annually and real output growth of 2.7% annually for 2024-2034, matching the d5 anchor and its disclosed broader-industry limitation.

WPP's 2025 annual report documents commissions, fees, incentives, retainers, labour as the primary input for most fee arrangements, pass-through treatment, short and cancellable contracts, and 2025 organic declines tied partly to client losses and spending cuts.

Capstone's April 2026 update reports rising marketing-services and advertising transaction activity, 68.6% strategic-buyer share, and sponsor preference for low concentration, recurring revenue, and AI-enabled offerings. It covers a broader marketing-services deal universe, as disclosed.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years and Census data showing 52.3% of employer businesses owned by people 55 or older. The source is all-industry and reports intentions, not completed agency control sales.
